description: A common bird throughout Belize, the Melodious Blackbird is often seen in towns, villages, and farmland. Males are entirely black with a glossy sheen, while females are duller brown-black.
funFact: Common in towns, villages, and open farmland; often on lawns, wires, and roadsides.
voice: Melodious, flute-like song often heard at dawn and dusk.

description: One of the most common hummingbirds in Central America. It has a green body, rufous tail, and straight red bill with a black tip.
funFact: Aggressive at feeders and flowers; often the default hummingbird in gardens and open woodland.
fieldMarks: Straight red bill with black tip
voice: Chipping calls and buzzy song from perches.

description: A bold and noisy flycatcher often found in pairs or small groups. Has a distinctive white eyebrow, dark cap, and yellow belly.
funFact: Social Flycatchers build their nests near wasp nests for protection from predators.
voice: Noisy chattering calls; often heard before seen.

description: A large, noisy jay with brown plumage, a pale belly, and a long tail. Often travels in small flocks through the forest canopy.
funFact: Travels in noisy flocks through forest edge and canopy; listen for harsh calls before looking up.
voice: Harsh, loud calls; often in noisy groups.

description: A small seedeater with sexual dimorphism: males have a distinctive black hood and white collar, while females are plain buffy-brown.
funFact: Look on grassy roadsides and weedy fields; males sing from fence wires and shrub tops.
fieldMarks: Male: black hood and white collar
voice: Dry buzzy trill sung from exposed perches.

description: A boldly patterned wren with heavy black spotting on white underparts and a distinctive barred tail often held upright.
funFact: Skulks in dense tangles at forest edge; often sings from thick cover when hard to see.
fieldMarks: Heavy black spots on white breast
voice: Loud musical song, frequently a duet between mated pairs.

description: The national bird of Belize! Unmistakable with its massive rainbow-colored bill, black plumage, yellow throat and chest, and red undertail.
funFact: Despite its large size, the bill is surprisingly light, made of hollow bone struts covered in keratin.

description: A skulking bird of the forest understory. Males are dull red with a brighter red throat patch, while females are olive-brown.
funFact: Follows army ant swarms and mixed flocks in the understory; look low in dense cover.
fieldMarks: Male: dull red with brighter red throat patch
voice: Sharp chips and churring calls from low vegetation.

description: A smaller relative of the toucan with a colorful bill, black head, yellow underparts with a black chest band, and red rump.
funFact: Aracaris often sleep communally, with up to six birds roosting together in a tree cavity.

description: A small, compact dove. Males are ruddy brown with a gray crown, while females are more uniformly gray-brown. Often seen on the ground.
funFact: Common on bare ground in villages and fields; flushes with whistling wings.
fieldMarks: Male: ruddy brown with gray crown
Small compact dove, often on ground
voice: Soft monotonous cooing in open areas.

description: A tiny, colorful finch. Males have blue-black upperparts with a bright yellow forehead and underparts, while females are olive-green.
funFact: Often in pairs at fruiting trees and mistletoe; listen for sweet whistled notes in canopy.
fieldMarks: Male: yellow throat and belly, blue-black upperparts

description: A large, thick-billed songbird with a distinctive black head, white throat, and olive-green back. Often heard singing from cover.
funFact: The name 'saltator' comes from Latin meaning 'dancer' or 'leaper'.

description: A tiny grassland bird. Males are entirely glossy blue-black, while females are plain brown. Males perform spectacular jumping displays.
funFact: Males jump straight up while singing from short grass and roadsides; watch open weedy areas.
voice: Insect-like buzzy song during vertical display jumps.

description: An all-black bird with a distinctive long tail and grooved, parrot-like bill. Often seen in small groups in open country.
funFact: Look for loose groups along roadsides and field edges; grooves on bill visible at close range.
voice: Whining and clicking calls from roadside groups.

description: A small, mostly green parakeet with an olive throat and blue in the wings. Often seen in noisy flocks flying overhead.
funFact: Scan open country and forest edge for fast-flying green flocks; nests in tree termitaria.
fieldMarks: Blue in wings visible in flight
voice: Harsh screeching calls; noisy flocks often heard before seen.

description: A pale blue-gray tanager with distinctive yellow wing patches visible in flight. Often found in pairs or small groups.
funFact: Frequently joins mixed-species flocks at forest edge, gardens, and open woodland.
fieldMarks: Yellow wing patches conspicuous in flight
voice: High thin chips; often in pairs or small groups.

description: A large, slender cuckoo with rufous upperparts, gray underparts, and a very long tail with white-tipped feathers.
funFact: Hops squirrel-like through mid-level branches; rusty back and long tail are key in flight.
fieldMarks: Very long tail with white-tipped feathers

description: A brown woodcreeper with heavy streaking on the head and underparts, and a pale ivory-colored, slightly curved bill.
funFact: Common on trunks in open woodland and edges; note large pale bill compared to smaller woodcreepers.
fieldMarks: Heavy streaking on head and underparts
Climbs trunks with stiff tail as prop
voice: Loud piercing whistles from trunks and limbs.

description: A distinctive bird with pale gray plumage, black face and cap, red bare skin around the eye, and a pink-based bill.
funFact: Perches conspicuously in clearings and canopy edges; black mask and red eye ring are distinctive.
fieldMarks: Red bare skin around eye, pink-based bill
voice: Whistles and wheezing calls from open perches.

description: A large flycatcher with a massive black bill, rufous-edged wings and tail, and yellow underparts. Similar to Social Flycatcher but larger.
funFact: Compare bill size to Social Flycatcher when seen together; favors woodland edge and riversides.
fieldMarks: Massive black bill (much larger than Social Flycatcher)
Rufous edges on wings and tail
voice: Loud harsh calls from exposed perches.

description: A small bird with a gray-blue crown and upper face, olive-green upperparts, whitish chin, pale gray throat, very pale lemon-yellow breast, and whitish belly with greenish yellow flanks.
funFact: Joins mixed-species flocks in mid-canopy; may hang upside-down while foraging.
fieldMarks: Gray-blue crown and pale lemon-yellow breast
voice: Short whistled phrases; often heard in mixed flocks.

description: A small flycatcher with a dark blackish gray crown, white eye-ring, sooty olive-gray back, dusky wings with two thin wing bars, white throat and chin, and white to yellowish white underparts.
funFact: Sits erect on exposed perches at forest edge; sallies out for insects and returns to same perch.
fieldMarks: Dark gray crown with white eye-ring
voice: Soft whistled pee-wee; tail shivers on landing.

description: Males have slate-black head, neck, and chest with a sky-blue eye ring, bright metallic bluish green to golden green upperparts, and cadmium to chrome yellow underparts. Females have dark slate upperparts instead of metallic green.
funFact: Perches quietly at mid-level in woodland; yellow belly and dark head pattern stand out in shade.
fieldMarks: Male: black head and chest, metallic green back, yellow belly
Female: dark slate upperparts, duller yellow below

description: Adults have a light bluish head and underparts, with darker blue upperparts and a shoulder patch colored a different shade of blue. Sexes are similar, but immature birds are much duller.
funFact: One of the most common tanagers around human habitation; look on fruiting trees and wires.
voice: High thin chips; confiding around gardens and villages.

description: Adults have metallic bronze to bronzy green upperparts, with white underparts and metallic bronzy green from the cheeks to the flanks. The tail is metallic bronze with darker bands.
funFact: Common at forest edge flowers; white belly separates it from mostly green hummingbirds.
fieldMarks: Bronzy green on cheeks and flanks
voice: High chips and buzzy song from perches.

description: Males have a yellow forehead, olive-green crown, nape, upperparts, and tail with a bluish gray gloss, and bright yellow underparts. Females have a rufous-chestnut forehead and mostly olive plumage with tawny-rufous underparts.
funFact: Look for pairs at mistletoe and fruiting trees in forest edge and secondary growth.
fieldMarks: Male: yellow forehead, olive upperparts, bright yellow underparts
Female: rufous forehead, olive plumage, tawny-rufous below
voice: Sweet whistled notes, often in pairs.

description: A very small wren with brownish plumage. The name troglodytes means hole dweller, referencing the bird's tendency to disappear into crevices when hunting insects or seeking shelter.
funFact: Common around buildings, gardens, and forest edge; explores crevices and tangles.
fieldMarks: Faint barring on wings and tail
voice: Bubbling energetic song; sharp chittering calls.

description: Has chestnut brown upperparts with a darker crown, pale eyebrows, black-and-white streaked sides of the head and neck, and white underparts becoming buff on the lower belly. Wings and very short tail are barred with black.
funFact: Stays low in humid forest understory; listen for duets when the bird is hard to see.
voice: Loud duetting song in dense forest; musical whistled phrases.

description: A very large woodpecker, 31.5-36 cm long. Mainly black above with a red crest and whitish lines from the bill down the neck and shoulders. Underparts are whitish heavily barred with black. Adult males have a red line from the bill to the throat and a red forehead, while these features are black in females. The call is a loud, ringing wic-wic-wic.
funFact: Our largest common woodpecker; look for white neck stripes and red crest at forest edge.
fieldMarks: Very large black woodpecker with red crest
White lines on neck and shoulders

description: A small songbird, 12.2 cm long. Males are brilliant violet-blue with black wings, tail and back, bright red legs, and a turquoise crown. Females and immatures are mainly green with paler, faintly streaked underparts. The call is a thin, high-pitched tsip. Often found in small groups feeding on insects, seeds, fruit, and nectar.
funFact: Males undergo a dramatic seasonal transformation, molting from their brilliant violet-blue breeding plumage into greenish eclipse plumage after the breeding season!
fieldMarks: Male: turquoise with black wings and red legs
voice: Sharp chips; male sings from canopy.

description: A medium-sized bird, 18.5-21 cm long. Adult males are mostly black with bright yellow patches on the shoulder, rump, belly, wing lining, and crissum. Females in the north have olive-yellow crown and upperparts with black face, throat, and wings; southern females resemble males. Both sexes have a slender, slightly decurved black bill with silvery base.
funFact: Favors gardens, plantations, and open woodland; yellow patches flash in flight.
fieldMarks: Male: mostly black with bright yellow shoulder, rump, and belly patches
Female: olive-yellow with black face and wings (northern form)
voice: Clear whistled phrases and chattering calls.

description: A colorful near-passerine bird with a black central crown surrounded by a blue band and a black eyemask. Features distinctive tail feathers with racket-shaped tips. The call is a low owl-like ooo-doot. Often sits motionless in dense forest habitat, making them difficult to spot despite their size. Eats insects, lizards, and fruit.
funFact: Often sits motionless in shaded forest; tail rattles are a key ID feature when seen.
fieldMarks: Blue crown band and black eye mask

description: A small tyrant flycatcher, 12.5-14 cm long. Has a dark olive crown, thin white eye-ring, and olive-green upperparts. Wings are dusky with yellowish-olive edges forming two wing bars. Throat is pale greenish gray, breast grayish greenish olive, and belly bright yellow. Features a wide flat bill with black upper mandible and pale gray lower mandible. Tends to be sluggish, peering slowly around from a perch.
funFact: Sluggish canopy flycatcher; peers slowly from mid-level perches in humid forest.
fieldMarks: Olive crown with thin white eye-ring

description: A very large woodpecker, 35.5-38 cm long. Both sexes have black upperparts with vertical white or buffy white stripes on the sides of the neck forming a 'V' on the back. Throat and upper breast black, rest of underparts light yellowish buff with narrow black bars. Long ivory yellow chisel-like bill. Males have a red head with bushy crest; females have black forecrown and center of crown.
funFact: Needs large trees; listen for squeaky toy calls in mature forest and mangroves.
fieldMarks: Very large with long ivory-yellow bill
Male: red crest; female: black crown center

description: A tiny flycatcher, 9-10 cm long. Has a black crown and nape, white supercilium, yellow iris, gray face and upperparts, bright yellow underparts, and a short black tail. The wings have two yellowish wing bars. Features a distinctive long, flat, black bill that is pale at the base of the lower mandible. Often perches sideways on vertical stems.
funFact: One of the smallest flycatchers; often perches sideways on vertical stems at forest edge.
fieldMarks: Tiny with black cap and white supercilium
voice: High sharp tseek or trilled calls.

description: A hummingbird, 13-16 cm long. Males weigh 5-7.5 g, females 4-6.5 g. Has greenish-brown upperparts and brownish to grayish underparts. Uppertail coverts have dark and light ochre bands, and central tail feathers are long with long white tips. Features a long decurved bill, with females having a shorter but more curved bill than males.
funFact: Forest understory hummer; follows trap-line of flowers along shaded trails.
fieldMarks: Long central tail feathers with white tips
voice: Chipping calls at forest leks; males sing persistently.

description: A colorful trogon, 23-25 cm long, weighing 38-57 g. Males have a violet-blue head, neck, and upper breast with black face and throat. A pale yellow ring surrounds the eye, and a narrow white band separates the upper breast from the bright yellow lower breast and belly. Upperparts are metallic green with a violet-blue tail. Females are gray above with duller yellow underparts.
funFact: Common at forest edge and clearings; compare violet head with Black-headed Trogon.
fieldMarks: Male: violet-blue head and upper breast, metallic green back, yellow belly
White band between breast and belly
Female: gray above, duller yellow below

description: A sparrow, 15-16 cm long, weighing 24-31 g. Has a mostly slate gray head with wide black stripes on each side of the crown, a black spot in front of the eye, and a long black stripe behind the eye. Upperparts, tail, and wings are bright olive green with canary yellow on the edge of the wings. Chin and throat are dull white, breast and sides grayish, belly white, and undertail coverts yellowish olive.
funFact: Forages on or near the ground at forest edge and scrub; olive back is distinctive.
fieldMarks: Slate gray head with black crown stripes
voice: Musical whistled song from low vegetation.

description: A small bird, 15.5-16.5 cm long, weighing 20-40 g. Has an indistinct pale ring around the eye on an otherwise dark brownish olive face. Upperparts and tail are dark brownish olive, wings are warmer brown. Throat and breast are brownish olive, belly and vent grayish olive. Both sexes have the same plumage.
funFact: Skulks in mid-story of humid forest; presence often detected by morning song.
voice: Clear rich whistles, mostly at dawn.

description: A bird, 15-18.5 cm long, weighing 24-32 g. Males are barred with alternating black and white throughout. Females have a cinnamon-rufous crest and upperparts with streaked black and white head and neck sides, buff chin, and ochraceous-buff underparts. Both sexes have a bushy crest and stout hooked bills.
funFact: Common in thickets and second growth; pairs defend territories year-round.
fieldMarks: Female: cinnamon crest, streaked head, buff underparts
voice: Loud duetting grating song; pairs pump tails while singing.

description: A small flycatcher, 13-15 cm long, weighing 8.5-17 g. Has greenish upperparts and yellowish underparts with a dusky wash on the chest. Features a white or yellow eye ring, white or yellowish wing bars that contrast against black wings, and a broad flat bill. Upper mandible is dark while lower mandible is orange-pink.
funFact: Migrates from breeding grounds in Canada and northeastern US to winter in southern Mexico and Central America. Makes a cup nest in sphagnum moss on or near the ground!
voice: Soft "chu-wee" call; breeds in North America, winters in Belize.

description: A medium-sized hummingbird, 11-12 cm long. Males weigh 7.4-9 g, females 6-9.2 g. Males are unmistakable with dark blue head and chest, white belly and tail with black tips, and a white band on the nape separating the blue head from the bright green back. Females are highly variable with green upperparts, blue-green throat and breast with white scales, white belly, and mostly green tail with blue end.
funFact: Conspicuous at canopy flowers; males are unmistakable in good light.
fieldMarks: Male: dark blue head and chest, white belly and nape band, green back
Female: variable; green above with scaled blue-green throat
voice: Sharp chips; aggressive at flowering trees.

description: A medium-sized parrot, 24 cm long, weighing 225-250 g. Has a mostly green plumage with a distinctive white forecrown and throat with bare pink skin. The crown ranges from deep white to bluish white, with a magenta spot on the throat and bronze-green to bluish-green ear coverts. Chest and belly are yellowish-olive with bluish edges, while the undertail coverts are red. Features dark blue flight feathers and tail with red undertail coverts.
funFact: Look for white crown and red undertail in fast-flying flocks over forest edge.
fieldMarks: Mostly green with red undertail coverts
voice: Harsh screeching calls; noisy flocks in flight.

description: One of the smallest woodcreepers, 13-19 cm long, with a slim body and short straight bill. Has mostly olivaceous plumage with rufous tail and flight feathers. Unlike most other woodcreepers, has no streaks or spots. Males weigh 9-18 g, females 8.6-16 g. Often seen hitching up tree trunks and along branches from mid-level to subcanopy.
funFact: Often with mixed flocks and ant swarms; plain olive plumage and rufous tail are key.

description: A hummingbird, 11.5-13.5 cm long. Males have a bright violet crown, metallic green to bronze green upperparts, and bluish green uppertail coverts. Face is dull grayish white to gray with a white spot behind the eye. Underparts are brownish gray. Females similar but with duller blue crown and pale cinnamon wash on underparts. Bill is straight, longish, and stout.
funFact: Listen in humid forest for long musical phrases from low perches.
fieldMarks: Male: bright violet crown, metallic green upperparts
voice: Complex whistled song for a hummingbird; sings from understory perches.

description: A small falcon, 23-30 cm long. Adults have blue-black head and upperparts with grayish edges. Throat, upper breast, and neck sides are white to buff; rest of breast is black with fine white bars. Belly, thighs, and undertail coverts are chestnut-rufous. Long wings and longish tail with square tip. Bright yellow cere and bare skin around eye, orange-yellow legs and feet.
funFact: Perches high on snags at forest edge; most active around dawn and dusk.
fieldMarks: Small falcon with long pointed wings

description: A small-medium woodpecker, 17-19 cm long, weighing 42-68 g. Males have golden yellow forehead and red crown/nape. Females have white forehead, black central crown, and red hindcrown/nape. Both have black around eye extending down neck with white line behind eye. Barred upper back, white lower back. Whitish throat and breast with olive-buff tinge, buffish white underparts with wavy bars and red belly spot.
funFact: Active in clearings and second growth; smaller and more colorful than Lineated Woodpecker.
fieldMarks: Black cheek patch with white line behind eye

description: A sexually dimorphic songbird. Males are dark blue with lighter blue eyebrows and shoulder patches on wings, and lighter blue forehead above beak. Females have dark brown plumage with slight reddish hue. Prefer to stay hidden in dense vegetation. Song consists of about six whistles with decreasing pitch.
funFact: Usually hidden in dense understory; learn the song to find it.
fieldMarks: Male: dark blue with lighter blue eyebrows and wing patches
voice: Series of about six whistles descending in pitch.

description: A large tyrant flycatcher, 18 cm long, weighing 40 g, with big head, hooked and slightly upturned bill, and upright stance. Head olive-green streaked with black, back chestnut or olive, rump bright yellow, tail brown. Wings dark brown with two pale wing bars. Whitish or yellow throat and yellow breast variably streaked darker. Belly white becoming yellow near tail. Red iris. Very variable plumage.
funFact: Noisy and aggressive in thickets; bright yellow rump visible when it flies.
fieldMarks: Large flycatcher with hooked upturned bill
voice: Loud beat-it, beat-it call and plaintive whistles.

description: A hummingbird 11.5-13 cm long, weighing 8-10 g. Mostly bronzy green with a grayish buff belly and distinctive scaly appearance created by buffy edges on underpart feathers. The tail is mostly bronzy with white tips on outer feathers. Has a mostly black bill with pink base.
funFact: Favors drier edge habitats and mangroves; scaly underpart pattern is distinctive.
fieldMarks: Gray-buff belly with scaly feather edges
Bronze tail with white tips on outer feathers
voice: Chips and buzzy display notes at leks.

description: A large pigeon, 26.5 cm long, weighing 150 g. Mainly wine-purple in color with a black bill and purple-red eyes and legs. The belly is browner and the back is more olive-brown, with blackish tail and flight feathers. Forages in tree tops for mistletoe, fruits and berries.
funFact: Forages in canopy for mistletoe and fruit; listen for calls before scanning treetops.
voice: Complex loud high-pitched calls slower than related pigeons.

description: A distinctive blackbird about 21.5 cm long. Both males and females are entirely black with a characteristic pale yellow bill that is chisel-shaped at the tip. Has a pale yellow iris and gray legs. Often feeds like a woodpecker, hammering and probing bamboo stems and rotten wood to extract insects.
funFact: Hammering into bamboo and rotten wood is a good clue; pairs often forage together in understory.
fieldMarks: Entirely black with pale yellow chisel-tipped bill
voice: Complex duetting vocalizations from dense cover.

description: A small bird, 10-10.7 cm long. Adult males have an olive-green back with black face and breast marked by a distinctive bright yellow throat, supercilia, and lower eyelid spot. The rest of the underparts are greyish olive. Females are dull olive-green above and paler grey below with a weaker yellow face pattern.
funFact: Common in weedy fields and roadsides; males sing from low grass and fence lines.
fieldMarks: Male: black face and breast with bright yellow throat and eyering
Female: duller with weak yellow face
voice: High buzzy trill from low perches in grass.

description: A thick-billed songbird found from Mexico to Panama. Members of the saltator genus have robust bills, relatively long tails, and strong legs. This species has grey upperparts with cinnamon-colored underparts. Like other saltators, it forages for seeds, fruits, and insects in the canopy and mid-levels of trees.
funFact: Look in canopy and mid-levels at forest edge and gardens; listen for rich whistled song.
voice: Musical whistled phrases; elaborate dawn song.

description: A medium-sized woodpecker, 18-23 cm long. Both sexes have mostly green upperparts with a bronze tinge and pale buffy yellow underparts barred with blackish olive. Males have a slate gray crown with a red border and nape and a wide red malar stripe. Females have red only on the nape. Forages from the mid-level to the canopy for ants, termites, and wood-boring beetles.
funFact: Mid-canopy woodpecker of forest edge and clearings; green back separates it from larger species.
fieldMarks: Mostly green upperparts with bronze tinge
Male: red nape and malar stripe
voice: Rolling calls and drumming on dead wood.

description: The mangrove swallow is a passerine bird found from Mexico through Central America to Panama. It has iridescent blue-green upperparts, white underparts, a white rump, and a black tail. It can be identified by the distinctive supraloral white streak near its eye, a feature shared only with two other Tachycineta species. Highly territorial when breeding, it nests in cavities near water and feeds on unusually large aerial prey for its size, including dragonflies and bees.
funFact: Scan rivers, lagoons, and mangroves for fast low flight; white supraloral streak is key.

description: A compact, short-tailed bird with a heavy hooked bill and orange legs. Males have a black crown, wings, and tail, a white head, neck, and breast contrasting with a bright yellow belly. Females are olive-green with a yellow belly. Males perform elaborate lekking displays on the forest floor, using their modified wings to produce loud snapping sounds.
funFact: Listen for snapping wings on forest floor display courts at forest edge.
fieldMarks: Male: black crown and wings, white head and breast, yellow belly
voice: Loud wing snaps at display courts; soft calls in understory.

description: A medium-sized bird of prey with creamy white underparts, a broad black face mask that wraps around the neck, and a small bushy crest. The wings and back are dusky black, and the tail has bold white bands. It has short, rounded wings and a long tail. Known for its loud, far-carrying laughing call, it is often seen perched conspicuously for hours watching for prey.
funFact: Often perched conspicuously for long periods; specializes on snakes including coral snakes.

description: A medium-sized flycatcher with a small head and bushy crest. It has brownish olive upperparts, whitish wing bars, a pale gray throat, olive gray breast, and yellow belly. Common in open and semi-open habitats from Mexico through Central and South America.
funFact: Common in open country, gardens, and savanna; crest and yellow belly visible at distance.

description: A very small hummingbird with dull iridescent green upperparts, pale rufous rump, buff belly, and a blackish "bandit-mask" face with whitish stripes. It has a long, decurved bill and is found in wooded habitats from Mexico through Central America to northwestern South America.
funFact: Understory hummer of edges and gardens; smaller and more masked than Long-billed Hermit.
fieldMarks: Very small with long decurved bill

description: A large, predominantly white vulture with gray to black ruff, flight, and tail feathers. The head and neck are bald with colorful skin in yellow, orange, blue, purple, and red, and it has a distinctive orange fleshy caruncle on its beak.
funFact: Soars high over forest; white wings and colorful head stand out against Turkey and Black vultures.
fieldMarks: Mostly white body with black flight feathers and tail
Colorful bare head (yellow, orange, red, purple)
voice: Mostly silent; occasional croaks at carcasses.

description: A very small finch with a stubby bill, about 10 cm long. Males have a yellow forecrown, glossy blue-black head and upperparts, and bright yellow underparts. Females are olive-yellow with grayish crowns. It is found in open and semi-open habitats from Mexico to Costa Rica.
funFact: Look at mistletoe and fruiting trees in scrub and forest edge.
fieldMarks: Male: yellow forecrown, blue-black head and back, yellow underparts
voice: Sweet whistled notes, often in pairs.

description: A large chestnut bird with a blackish head and rump, bright yellow tail, bare blue cheek patch, pink wattle, and a long black-and-red bill. Males are nearly twice the size of females. It is found in the Caribbean lowlands from Mexico to central Panama.
funFact: Look for hanging nest colonies in large trees; males much larger than females.
fieldMarks: Chestnut body with black head and rump
Bare blue cheek patch and pink wattle
voice: Bizarre liquid gurgling display calls from colonies.

description: A very small flycatcher, 9.5-10.5 cm long, with a distinctive downcurved bill. It has a pale gray head, olive upperparts, pale gray throat and breast with olive streaks, and a pale lemon-yellow belly. It inhabits dense undergrowth from Mexico to Panama.
funFact: Skulks low in thickets; bent bill is visible when it briefly perches mid-height.
fieldMarks: Very small with distinctive downcurved bill
voice: Rapid toad-like trill from dense undergrowth.

description: A medium-sized gray songbird, 23 to 25.5 cm long, with a whitish eyebrow, dark stripe through the eye, off-white underparts, and blackish wings with two white wing bars. It has a long dark tail with white feather tips and a slim slightly curved black bill. Common in open habitats and around human habitation throughout Belize and Central America.
funFact: Common around towns and open country; runs on ground and sings from wires and rooftops.
fieldMarks: Gray overall with white eyebrow and wing bars
Long dark tail with white tips
voice: Varied mimicry and repeated phrases; often sings from exposed perches.

description: A small antbird, 13 to 15 cm long. Males are mostly slate gray with a white patch between the scapulars and white tips on the wing coverts and outer tail feathers. Females have tawny-tinged olive-gray upperparts and tawny-buff underparts. Both sexes have distinctive rich chocolate-brown irises. Inhabits dense understorey of forest edges, clearings, and secondary growth from Mexico through Central America.
funFact: Follows army ant swarms and attends forest edge tangles; white scapular patch on male is distinctive.
fieldMarks: Male: slate gray with white scapular patch
voice: Sharp chips and duetting song at forest edge.

description: A thrush-like forest bird, 15 to 19 cm long, with a dusky brown crown, bare bluish skin around the eye, black face and throat, rufous sides of the neck, and uniform gray underparts. Walks slowly on the forest floor with its tail cocked, found in humid forests from sea level to 1,800 m from Mexico to Honduras.
funFact: Walks slowly on forest floor with tail cocked; more often heard than seen.
voice: Long whistled song, often at dawn.

description: A very small bird, 12 to 13 cm long, with a notably long thin bill and a short frequently cocked tail. Upperparts are grey-brown with rufous on the sides of the head, white throat shading to buff underparts, and a black tail with white tips. Forages actively in vegetation for insects and spiders.
funFact: Active in understory tangles at forest edge; long bill and cocked tail help ID.
fieldMarks: Very small with notably long thin bill
voice: Buzzing and rattling song from dense tangles.

description: A large trogon, 30 to 35.5 cm long. Males have an orange-red bill, metallic green upperparts and breast, deep red belly, and slate gray undertail. Females are gray where males are green. Often seen sitting motionless in the forest midstory, from Mexico through Central America to Colombia and Ecuador.
funFact: Sits quietly in midstory of humid forest; red belly and slaty tail separate from smaller trogons.
fieldMarks: Male: metallic green upperparts, red belly, slate gray undertail
Female: gray where male is green

description: A small flycatcher, 12.5 to 14 cm long, with greenish olive upperparts, buffy ochraceous edges on the wings, and rich cinnamon buff underparts. Forages at middle levels of the forest, hover-gleaning fruit and insects from a perch. Found from Mexico through Central America to northern South America.
funFact: Forages at middle levels, hover-gleaning fruit and insects; warm buff belly is distinctive.
voice: Soft whistled notes from mid-level perches.

description: A tiny bird, just 10 cm long. Males are velvety black with a brilliant red cap and bright yellow thighs. Females are olive green above with paler greenish-yellow underparts. Found in the understory of humid forests from Mexico to Ecuador, often near fruiting trees.
funFact: Male displays on low branches in humid forest; red cap flashes in dark understory.
fieldMarks: Male: velvety black with brilliant red cap and yellow thighs
voice: Soft calls; wing snaps at leks.

description: A medium-sized warbler, about 13 cm long, with olive upperparts and bright yellow underparts. The male has a gray crown and nape with black restricted to the lores and behind the eye, unlike the full face mask of other yellowthroats. The female is similar but lacks the black face markings. Found in open grassy and shrubby habitats from Mexico to Panama.
funFact: Favors grassy fields and scrub; perches low in open habitats.
fieldMarks: Male: gray crown, black only at lores and behind eye
voice: Sharp chack calls; musical song from grass and shrubs.

description: A small flycatcher, 13-14 cm long, with greenish olive upperparts, a concealed yellow crown stripe, and pale yellow underparts. It has a whitish gray supercilium and partial white eyering. Found in a wide variety of wooded habitats from Mexico to Argentina.
funFact: Common in varied wooded habitats; crown stripe may flash when excited.

description: A large pigeon, 30-32 cm long, with a dull purple head and breast, copper gloss on the nape, and pale grey underparts. The bill is black and the legs, iris, and eyering are red. Found from southern Mexico to northern Argentina in forest edges and partially open areas.
funFact: Look along rivers and forest edge; pale gray belly separates it from darker pigeons.

description: A large kingfisher, about 30 cm long, with dark bronzy green upperparts, a white collar, and a long heavy black bill. Males have a rich rufous breast while females are green across the breast. Found from southern Mexico to northern Argentina along rivers and lakes.
funFact: Perches over rivers and lagoons; may hover before plunging for fish.
fieldMarks: Male: rufous breast; female: green breast

description: A striking raptor, 46-56 cm long, with entirely white head, body, and underwings contrasting with black upper wings and a short black tail with a broad white band. The bill is black and legs are yellow. Females are larger than males. Found from southern Mexico through Central America to the Amazon basin.
funFact: Soars over forest and follows monkey troops to catch flushed prey; unmistakable when seen well.
fieldMarks: Mostly white head, body, and underwings
Short black tail with broad white band

description: A large rail, 33-40 cm long, with slate gray crown and hindneck, a chestnut patch on the nape, grayish olive back, white chin, and pale tawny cinnamon underparts fading to black on the lower belly. It has a thick dark yellow bill, deep red eye, and red legs. Found from Mexico to Costa Rica in dense wetlands and forests.
funFact: Skulks in dense wetlands and mangroves; often heard at dusk rather than seen.
voice: Rapid popping and clucking song at twilight.

description: A tiny owl, only about 15 cm long, stocky with disproportionately large feet and talons. Its crown has elongated white streaks and its underparts are heavily streaked white. Two dark spots on the nape resemble false eyes. Color varies from grey-brown to rich rufous. Found in semi-open wooded habitats from the southern US to Argentina.
funFact: Diurnal and often mobbed by songbirds; listen for toots at forest edge and scan for mobbing flocks.

description: A large parrot, 38-41 cm long, mainly green with a distinctive whitish powdery tinge on the back and nape, a short square tail, and a red speculum on the wing. It has a broad white eye-ring and a bi-colored tail that is paler yellow at the tip. Found in humid forests from southern Mexico to the Amazon basin.
funFact: Large green parrot of humid forest; whitish nape and red speculum visible in flight.
fieldMarks: Large green parrot with whitish powdery nape
voice: Harsh screeching calls; noisy flocks in flight.

description: A striking wetland bird, 21-24 cm long, with a chestnut-maroon body, black head and neck, bright yellow bill, and a yellow fleshy forehead shield. Its most remarkable feature is its extremely long toes that allow it to walk on floating lily pads. Females are significantly larger than males. Found from Mexico to Panama in freshwater wetlands.
funFact: Walks on floating vegetation in freshwater wetlands; long toes and yellow shield are unmistakable.
voice: Metallic notes and calls from lily pads.

description: The white-whiskered puffbird is about 18 cm (7.1 in) long. Both sexes have bristles around the base of the large bill and distinctive white 'whiskers' made of feather tufts. Males have brown to chestnut brown upperparts while females have grayer crowns and olive to brown upperparts.
funFact: Sits motionless on low perches at forest edges; watch for sudden darting sallies after insects and small prey.
fieldMarks: White whisker tufts at bill base
voice: Low whistled notes from a perch.

description: The crimson-collared tanager averages 19-20 cm (7.5-8 in) long. Adult plumage is black with a vivid red collar covering the nape, neck, and breast, and red tail coverts. Its bill is striking pale blue and legs are blue-gray. Females average slightly duller than males but are sometimes indistinguishable.
funFact: Often in pairs at forest edges and clearings; the crimson collar and rump flash when it flies.
fieldMarks: Black body with crimson collar and rump
voice: Sharp chip notes, often in pairs.

description: The bare-throated tiger heron is 80 cm (31 in) long, a large wading bird with a bare greenish-yellow to orange throat. Adults have a black crown, light grey sides of the head, and blackish upperparts narrowly barred with buff. Juveniles are buff coarsely barred with black. Its flight is heavy and it is often found motionless along river and lake banks.
funFact: Stands motionless along river and lake banks; listen for the booming call at dusk from gallery forest.

description: The rufous-tailed jacamar is 25 cm (10 in) long with a 5 cm (2 in) black bill. It is metallic green above with mainly orange underparts and a green breast band. Males have a white throat while females have a buff throat. It sits with its bill tilted up, flying out to catch insects in mid-air.
funFact: Perches horizontally with bill tilted up at woodland edges; sallies out for flying insects.
fieldMarks: Metallic green above, rufous tail and belly
Male white throat; female buff throat

description: A large seed-eating bird with a black face, yellow head, neck and breast, and olive back, wings and tail. It inhabits the canopy and middle levels of dense wet forests and tall second growth in Central American lowlands.
funFact: Noisy flocks of up to 20 birds often mix with honeycreepers and tanagers at fruiting trees.
fieldMarks: Black face, yellow head and breast

description: A powerful eagle with black plumage, barred wings, and a long narrow tail with distinctive grey bars. It hunts relatively large prey including monkeys, rodents, opossums, and reptiles from central Mexico through Central America to South America.
funFact: Soars high over rainforest; scan the sky above primary forest for the long barred tail.

description: A striking tyrant flycatcher with white underparts, gray upperparts, a black cap, and an extremely long forked tail. Found from southern Mexico to Argentina in open habitats including pastures, savannas, and forest edges.
funFact: Open pastures and savannas; tail streamers make it unmistakable even at a distance in flight.
fieldMarks: Gray back, white underparts, black cap

description: A small, robust bird with a black conical bill. Males vary in plumage by subspecies from entirely black to black with white collars and bellies, while females are olive-brown above and paler below. Commonly found in semi-open areas from southern Mexico through Central America.
funFact: Belize males (S. c. corvina) are all black except a white wing patch; common in gardens and weedy edges.
fieldMarks: Belize male all black with small white wing patch
voice: Sweet whistled song from exposed perch.

description: A large, secretive forest falcon with a dark back, white neck and breast, and long tail. It has three color morphs (pale, tawny, and dark) and is a powerful predator that hunts mammals, birds, and reptiles in dense tropical forests from Mexico to South America.
funFact: Hear the eerie call at dawn from dense forest; rarely seen despite being vocally common.
fieldMarks: Long tail, dark back, white collar
voice: Low human-like ow or ahr call.

description: A small tanager with a long decurved bill. Males are mainly blue-tinged green with a black head and bright yellow bill, while females are grass-green without the black head. Found in the forest canopy from southern Mexico to Brazil.
funFact: Canopy frugivore; male black hood and decurved bill distinguish it from similarly colored tanagers.
fieldMarks: Male blue-green with black head, yellow bill

description: A large wren with a brown-grey crown, black nape, and heavily barred upperparts and tail with black, tawny, and white. The breast is white with heavy black spots and the belly is cinnamon. Found from Mexico to Ecuador.
funFact: Noisy family groups in scrub and forest edge; bold barred back and rufous underparts distinguish from other wrens.
voice: Loud musical duets, often in pairs.

description: A slender swift with long narrow wings and a long forked tail, mainly black with a white throat and upper breast. Found from southern Mexico through Central America and South America, often flying very fast and high above the forest canopy.
funFact: Flies fast and high above forest; white throat patch helps separate it from other dark swifts.
fieldMarks: Black with white throat and upper breast

description: A medium-sized hummingbird with a slightly decurved black bill. Males have metallic bronze-green upperparts and a velvety black stripe down the throat and chest, while females have white-tipped outer tail feathers. Found from Mexico through Central America in semi-open habitats.
funFact: Gardens and semi-open edges; male velvety black throat and chest stripe is distinctive in good light.
fieldMarks: Male bronze-green with velvety black chest stripe

description: A seed-eating tanager with a slate-grey head, white supercilium, olive-green upperparts, and a buff throat edged with black. Its thick convex black bill is distinctive. Found from southeastern Mexico through Central America to western Ecuador and northeastern Brazil.
funFact: Pairs sing antiphonal duets from understory at forest edges; thick bill is saltator hallmark.

description: A tiny flycatcher with a slate-grey crown, white forehead, bright olive upperparts, and bright yellow underparts with faint olive streaks on the breast. Found in the canopy and edges of humid forests from southern Mexico to western Panama.
funFact: Active leaf-gleaning in canopy; warbler-like foraging unlike typical sit-and-wait flycatchers.

description: A small flycatcher with brown upperparts, white throat, yellow breast, a short stubby tail, and a broad flat bill. It lacks a crown, which distinguishes it from other Platyrinchus species. Found in the undergrowth of tropical forests from southern Mexico to Costa Rica.
funFact: Forest understory specialist; flat spade bill and nearly absent crown separate it from other spadebills.
fieldMarks: No crown patch (unique in genus here)

description: A striking oriole that is mostly yellow with a black back, lower face, and upper breast, and black wings with yellow epaulets. The tail is black with yellow margins above and appears almost entirely yellow from below. It is the only oriole with prominent yellow in the tail.
funFact: Only oriole with prominent yellow in the tail; often in open country with scattered trees.
fieldMarks: Mostly yellow with black back and wings
Yellow margins in tail (unique among orioles)
Black lower face and upper breast

description: A small dove with striking sexual dimorphism. Males are blue-grey with black-spotted wings and paler underparts. Females are grey-brown with a ruddy brown back, chestnut rump and tail, and chestnut-brown wing spots. Both have red or yellow irises and a green bare eyering.
funFact: Pairs flush from forest floor; a blue-gray and brown bird flying together through understory is diagnostic.
fieldMarks: Female brown with chestnut tail and wing spots
Red or yellow iris, green eyering

description: A medium-sized woodcreeper with a brown head, buffy supercilium, bare gray skin around the eye, dark olive-brown upperparts, cinnamon-rufous tail, and tawny flight feathers. Its underparts are pale buff on the throat becoming cinnamon on the belly. It has a straight bill and a ruffled nape.
funFact: Near-obligate army-ant follower; look for it hopping near ant swarms on the forest floor.

description: A large sparrow with a chestnut crown streaked with black, brown upperparts with bold black streaks, chestnut wings and tail, and grayish buff underparts with a dark stripe on the side of the throat. Both sexes share the same plumage. Found from Mexico to Costa Rica.
funFact: Large sparrow of scrub and forest edge in the north; sings from exposed perches.

description: A medium-to-large raptor with a striking rufous cowl, black crown and long erectile crest, bold black barring on white underparts, and blackish upperparts with white-tipped shoulders. Juveniles are mostly white-headed with dark brown upperparts. Found in tall primary forests from Mexico to Argentina.
funFact: Primary rainforest raptor; crest and rufous nape visible when perched below a canopy gap.
fieldMarks: Rufous cowl and long erectile crest
Bold black barring on white underparts

description: A small cardinalid with striking sexual dimorphism. The male is brilliant blue with a sky blue forehead and cheek, blackish blue upperparts, and deep blue underparts. The female is dull brownish overall with a reddish tinge to the belly. Found in dense vegetation from Mexico to Nicaragua.
funFact: Skulks in dense tangles; male electric blue is startling when it sings from a low perch.
fieldMarks: Male deep blue with sky-blue forehead
Female plain brown with reddish belly

description: A medium-sized owl with a round head and no ear tufts. The crown, nape, and back are mottled brown, the facial disc is pale brown, and the underparts are off-white with bold vertical brown streaks. The eyes are dark brown. Found in a variety of wooded habitats from Mexico to Argentina.
funFact: Calls from woodland edge at night; dark eyes help separate from many other Neotropical owls.
fieldMarks: Bold vertical streaks on whitish underparts

description: A small sparrow-sized crake with bright chestnut plumage, a gray head with blackish crown, dark grey ear-coverts, and paler chin and belly. It has a short black conical bill, red iris, and distinctive olive-green legs and feet. Found in wet lowlands from Mexico to northwestern Costa Rica.
funFact: Wet marsh edges; bright rufous bird darting through vegetation—listen for rattling calls.
voice: Sharp rattling calls from dense cover.

description: A small greenlet with a yellowish forehead, yellow-brown crown, olive-brown upperparts, dull gray face, and ochraceous breast becoming bright yellow on the belly. It has a pale gray iris and moves actively through the forest understory, often hanging upside-down while foraging. Found from Mexico to Ecuador.
funFact: Understory acrobat often hangs upside-down; ochre crown and gray face separate it from other greenlets.

description: A striking tanager with strong sexual dimorphism. The male is mostly yellow to orange-yellow with a black hood, wings, and tail. The female is olive-yellow below and olive-green to brownish above with a grayish head. Both have a relatively large, hooked bill. Found from southeastern Mexico to central Honduras.
funFact: Aggressive canopy tanager with shrike-like bill; male black hood is bold in good light.
fieldMarks: Male yellow-orange with black hood, wings, and tail

description: A very small swift with a cigar-shaped body, long crescentic wings, and a short bluntly squared-off tail. The head, upperparts, and wings are dusky black, while the underparts, rump, and tail coverts are greyish brown with a paler grey throat. Found from Alaska through Central America to Venezuela, often flying in flocks over forests and open areas.
funFact: Winters in Belize in flocks over forest and towns; smaller and browner than White-collared Swift.

description: The largest hummingbird in Mexico and Central America at 13-15 cm. Males have a dusky crown with bluish green gloss, metallic violet blue underparts, and metallic green lower back. Females have metallic green upperparts, a violet blue throat, and gray underparts with a whiter belly. Found in humid evergreen forests and gardens from Mexico to Panama.
funFact: Largest regional hummer; males aggressively defend flower patches in humid forest and gardens.

description: A small warbler about 12.7 cm long with plain olive-gray upperparts, a bright yellow chest and throat, and a white belly. Its distinctive facial pattern includes a rufous cap, white eyebrow-line, dark eye-line, and white malar marking. It has a stout bill, round stubby wings, and a long tail often held at a high angle.
funFact: Pine-oak scrub and thickets in the north; cocked tail and loud chipping song reveal this skulker.
fieldMarks: Rufous cap, white eyebrow, dark eyeline
Yellow throat and chest, white belly
voice: Rapid accelerating chip series (not a warble).

description: The smallest woodcreeper at 13-16 cm, with a distinctive short upturned wedge-shaped bill. It has a russet-brown crown, rich brown upperparts, cinnamon-rufous rump and tail, and olive-brown underparts with fulvous spots and streaks on the breast. It hitches up tree trunks in the lower to middle levels of the forest, often in mixed-species flocks.
funFact: Tiny woodcreeper in mixed flocks; upturned wedge-shaped bill is unique among local woodcreepers.
fieldMarks: Fulvous spots and streaks on breast

description: A small, secretive ground bird about 21-24 cm long with unbarred sooty-brown plumage, a greyish head, whitish foreneck, and cinnamon-buff underparts. It has a very short tail and can be grey, olive, or yellow legs. More often heard than seen, it inhabits tropical lowland forests from southern Mexico through Central America to South America.
funFact: Walks quietly on forest floor; listen for soft whistles at dawn and dusk in humid lowland forest.
voice: Low mournful whistles, more often heard than seen.

description: A very small dove about 14.5-16 cm long, possibly the smallest columbid by mass. It has dark grey to brown plumage, paler wings with dark violet spots, rufous underwings, a grey bill, pink legs, and reddish eyes. Unlike the similar common ground dove, it lacks scaling on the head and neck. Found in open dry areas from southern Mexico through Central and South America.
funFact: Open dry grassland and scrub; lacks the scaled neck pattern of Ruddy Ground-Dove.
fieldMarks: Very small dove, possibly smallest by mass
Plain brown head and breast (no scaling)
Dark violet wing spots, rufous underwings

description: A large raptor 56-64 cm long with very broad wings, mainly black plumage, a short white tail with a broad black tip, a black bill, and yellow legs and cere. Immature birds are dark brown above with buff underparts and dark spots. Its call is a distinctive piping ooo-wheeeeee. Found from Mexico through Central America to northern Argentina.
funFact: Perches along rivers and coast; white tail base and black tip visible in flight.
fieldMarks: Short white tail with broad black tip
Yellow legs and cere, very broad wings

description: A small antbird 10-12.5 cm long with a long graduated tail. Males are mostly black with a concealed white patch between the shoulders and white spots on the wing coverts. Females have a blackish gray head and upperparts, black throat, and rufous-chestnut underparts. Found actively foraging in vine tangles and forest edges from Mexico through Central America to South America.
funFact: Pairs forage actively in vine tangles at forest edge; male white wing spots are distinctive.
fieldMarks: Male black with white wing dots
Female gray above, black throat, rufous underparts
Long graduated tail, active in vine tangles

description: A very small tyrant flycatcher 9-10 cm long with a gray crown and nape, olive back and tail, yellowish wing bars, and grayish white underparts with streaks on the throat and breast. Found in dense scrubby vegetation at forest edges and in secondary growth from Mexico through Central America to northern South America.
funFact: Dense scrub at forest edge and secondary growth; smaller and grayer-crowned than Common Tody-Flycatcher.
fieldMarks: Tiny flycatcher, gray crown and nape
voice: High thin calls from dense scrub.

description: A small furnariid 11-13 cm long with dull brown upperparts, a conspicuous buff supercilium, wide white malar stripe, and extensive rufous in the wings and tail. Its underparts are plain dull grayish brown with light spotting on the foreneck and breast. It has a distinctive wedge-shaped, slightly upturned bill. Found from southern Mexico through Central America to northern South America.
funFact: Chisels dead branches like a nuthatch, often hanging upside-down; buff eyebrow and rufous wings help ID.
voice: Sharp notes while foraging on branches.

description: A small hummingbird 7.5-9.3 cm long. Males have brilliant golden green upperparts and underparts, a red bill with black tip, and a long forked blue-black tail. Females have metallic green upperparts, light gray underparts, and a grayish white stripe behind the eye. Found in semi-open landscapes from Mexico through Central America.
funFact: Semi-open edges and gardens; wags partly spread tail while feeding at small flowers.
fieldMarks: Red bill with black tip, long forked tail
Female gray below with pale eye stripe

description: The smallest motmot at 16.5-18 cm long. It has a green crown, rufous neck, green back and rump, a blue supercilium, and a black mask with a white stripe below. Its throat is white, breast greenish with light streaks, and belly white. Found in humid evergreen forest from southern Mexico through Central America into Colombia.
funFact: Humid forest understory; tiny motmot with tody-like proportions—listen for soft hoots.
fieldMarks: Green crown, rufous neck, blue supercilium
Black mask with white stripe below

description: A medium-sized passerine 17-19 cm long. The male is dull reddish brown with a brighter red throat and breast, featuring a black-bordered scarlet crown stripe that is raised when excited. The female is yellowish brown with a yellow throat and yellow-buff crown stripe. Found from Mexico through Central America to Paraguay and northern Argentina.
funFact: Regular army-ant follower in understory; raised scarlet crown stripe visible when agitated.
fieldMarks: Male dull red-brown with scarlet crown stripe
Female yellow-brown with buff crown stripe
voice: Harsh chattering calls near ant swarms.

description: The largest swift in its range at 20-21.5 cm long with a 45-55 cm wingspan. Mostly black plumage with a distinctive white collar that circles the neck, broadest on the breast. The tail is slightly forked. Found from Mexico through Central America and the Caribbean to South America.
funFact: Huge fast-flying flocks over forest and towns; white collar visible even at distance.

description: A tyrant flycatcher 15-17 cm long with olive-green upperparts, a bold white eye-ring, and a large wide flat bill. The throat and upper breast are dusky olive-green becoming paler with yellowish streaks on the lower breast, with a pale yellow belly. Found from Mexico through Central America into Colombia.
funFact: Midstory flycatcher with conspicuous eye-ring; wide flat bill separates from other flycatchers.

description: A hummingbird 10-11.5 cm long with a bright metallic blue crown, metallic bronze nape and back, and white underparts with metallic bronze green sides to the breast. Found from Mexico through Belize, Guatemala, El Salvador into Nicaragua.
funFact: Pine-oak foothills and forest edge in the north; blue crown gleams in good light.

description: A tiny songbird with blue-gray upperparts, whitish underparts, and a long black tail with white outer feathers that is often held cocked. Males have a black cap with a striking white eyebrow; females have a blue-gray cap with a white eyebrow. Constantly in motion while foraging. Found from Mexico to northwestern Peru.
funFact: Hyperactive pairs in scrub and forest edge; tail often cocked while gleaning insects.
fieldMarks: Male black cap with white eyebrow
Long black tail with white outer feathers

description: A striking raptor 51-61 cm long with a white head, neck and body, black wings with a white leading edge, and a brownish tail barred black-grey with a white tip. It has a small black crest on top of the head and black around the eyes. Found from southern Mexico to northern Argentina.
funFact: Soars over tall forest; unmistakable black-and-white pattern unlike any other local raptor.
fieldMarks: Black wings with white leading edge

description: A spinetail 13-16 cm long with sepia brown upperparts, chestnut wings and tail, a slate black throat with white streaks, and a deep cinnamon rufous breast and sides. Found in dense vegetation from Mexico through Belize, Guatemala, El Salvador, and Honduras.
funFact: Dense scrub and edge tangles; rufous breast and streaked black throat are distinctive.
fieldMarks: Sepia brown upperparts, chestnut wings and tail

description: A slender hummingbird 9-13 cm long with bright emerald green upperparts, pure white underparts, and a long pointed tail with blue-black central and white outer feathers. The male has a metallic violet forecrown. Found from Mexico through Central America to northern Peru.
funFact: Canopy hummer; long white-edged tail and violet crown (male) visible at forest gaps.

description: A large woodcreeper 25-29 cm long with a cinnamon-brown face, rufescent crown, olive-brown back with black barring, cinnamon-rufous tail, and golden to cinnamon-buff underparts with narrow black barring. Has a long tail and medium-length straight bill. Found from Mexico through Central America to Colombia and Ecuador.
funFact: Army-ant follower on vertical trunks; larger and more heavily barred than Olivaceous Woodcreeper.
fieldMarks: Barred olive-brown back and buff underparts

description: A medium-sized woodcreeper 17.5-20.5 cm long with dark rufous to chestnut-brown upperparts, a brighter reddish crown, rufous chestnut wings and tail, cinnamon-buff throat, and rufous brown underparts with a paler belly. Has a straight bill and short tail. Found from Mexico to northern Colombia and Venezuela.
funFact: Army-ant follower clinging to trunks; all-rufous plumage unlike barred or olive woodcreepers.

description: A large dove 23.5-26 cm long with a pale bluish gray forehead and crown, pinkish buff face, white throat and belly, and pale pink breast. Upperparts are olive brown with a rufous wash. Outer tail feathers are black with white tips. Found from Mexico to Colombia.
funFact: Large understory dove; pale gray head and white-tipped tail flash when flushed from forest floor.
fieldMarks: Olive-brown upperparts, black tail with white tips

description: A lowland tanager with yellowish olive plumage over much of its body and a conspicuous gray head and crest. Has a somewhat conical bill with a rounded notch on the upper mandible. Usually travels in pairs in dense forest understory. Found from Mexico to Paraguay.
funFact: Understory pairs often follow army ants; gray crested head stands out in dim forest.

description: A woodpecker 21.5-25 cm long with rufous-chestnut upperparts, a cinnamon-buff rump, black bars on the back, and chestnut underparts heavily marked with black V shapes. The head is dull ochraceous with a pointed crest. Male has bright red from the lores to behind the eye and down to the throat. Found from Mexico to Panama.
funFact: Humid forest and mangroves; male red face and throat; heavily marked chestnut underparts.
fieldMarks: Rich chestnut body with black V marks below
Black bars on back, pointed crest
Male red from lores to throat

description: A small vireo 11-12 cm long with greenish brown upperparts, yellowish white wing bars, a whitish yellow chin, and grayish underparts with a faint yellow tinge on the belly. Found in Mexico and Central America.
funFact: Coastal mangroves and scrub; dull vireo best found by song in red mangrove stands.
fieldMarks: Grayish underparts with faint yellow belly

description: A small wren 9.5-10 cm long with medium brown upperparts, a gray-white supercilium, gray throat and chest, white belly, and buff flanks. The tail has many thin dark bars. Found in Mexico, Belize, Guatemala, and Honduras.
funFact: Thorny scrub and dry forest edge; white belly and gray breast separate from other small wrens.
fieldMarks: White belly, gray throat and chest

description: A raptor 35-40 cm long with a black head, bluish black upperparts, white throat, buffy rufous orange upper breast, and a black lower breast with coarse reddish brown bars. The belly and thighs are buffy rufous orange. Females are significantly larger than males. Found from Mexico through Central America to South America.
funFact: Rare cliff and emergent-tree nester; orange breast and heavy build unlike smaller Bat Falcon.
fieldMarks: Black lower breast with reddish bars

description: A puffbird about 25 cm long, mostly glossy black with a broad white forehead, throat, upper breast, and belly. A broad black band separates the white upper breast from the white belly, and the lower flanks are barred black and white. Has a very large black bill. Found from Mexico through Central America to South America.
funFact: More open than White-whiskered; bold black-and-white pattern visible on exposed snags.
fieldMarks: Glossy black with broad white neck and breast
Black band between white upper breast and belly

description: A large ground bird 38-46 cm long, olive-green with a whitish throat and belly, flanks barred black, and a rufous crown and neck. Has blue-grey legs and a distinctive three-note piping call heard at dusk. Found from Mexico through Central America to South America.
funFact: Listen for the three-note whistle at dusk in primary forest; usually walks rather than flies.
fieldMarks: Olive-green with rufous crown and neck
Whitish throat and belly, barred flanks

description: A dove 22.5-28 cm long with a pinkish gray forehead and face, dark brown crown and nape, iridescent grayish purple hindneck, olive-brown upperparts, white throat, reddish gray breast, and reddish belly. Has a yellow eye surrounded by bare gray skin and red legs. Found from Mexico to Colombia.
funFact: Secondary forest understory; reddish underparts and yellow eye distinguish from Gray-headed Dove.
fieldMarks: Pinkish gray face, dark brown crown
Yellow eye with bare gray skin

description: A tanager 13 cm long. The male has a golden head with a black eyemask edged with violet blue, black upperparts with turquoise shoulders, rump and wing edges, blue flanks, and a white central belly. The female has a greenish tinge to the head. Found from Mexico to Ecuador.
funFact: Canopy and garden edges; golden head and turquoise shoulders flash in mixed flocks with Blue-gray Tanagers.
voice: High thin chips in mixed flocks.

description: About 18 cm long with olive-brown upperparts, a thin white supercilium, black moustache stripe, and olive-brown underparts. The male has a red crown from forehead to nape; the female has a dark brown crown. Inhabits a wide variety of wooded landscapes from Mexico to Argentina.
funFact: Midstory woodpecker in varied forest types; plain brown plumage with red crown on male.
fieldMarks: Male red crown; female dark crown

description: A mid-sized, slender raptor 38-42 cm (15-16.5 in) long with a large, strongly hooked bill. Extreme variation in plumage: males appear blackish or gray from below, while females are brown or brick-red. Specializes in feeding on tree snails, using its hooked bill to pry open the shells.
funFact: Snail specialist of forest and wetlands; oversized hooked bill is the key field mark.
fieldMarks: Male dark below; female brown or brick-red

description: A small parrot 21-23 cm (8-9 in) long with a reddish brown hood, pinkish red patch behind the eye, and white eye ring. The nape and breast are dull olive-yellow with red flanks, and most of the body is green with blue on the wing edge. Found from Mexico to Colombia.
funFact: Small parrot of humid forest canopy; brown hood and red eye patch visible in flight.
fieldMarks: Green body with blue wing edge

description: A medium-sized passerine 16.5-20.5 cm (6.5-8 in) long. The male has a black crown, whitish-gray upperparts, black wings and tail, and white underparts. The female has a black cap on a chestnut face with buffy forehead. Found from Mexico to South America.
funFact: Fruit-eater of clearings and canopy; male black cap and pink bill visible on exposed perches.

description: A small wren 12.5-14 cm (5-5.5 in) long with a dark gray-brown crown, rufous-brown back, orange-rufous rump, and white supercilium. The throat is white, chest pale grayish buff, and belly buffy white. Found from Mexico to Costa Rica.
funFact: Garden and forest edge wren; orange rump and gray crown help separate from House Wren.

description: A small flycatcher 12.5-18 cm (5-7 in) long with an erectile fan-shaped crest. The male's crest is red with blue tips; the female's is yellow or orange. Upperparts are dark brown with narrow black and buffy bars, rump is cinnamon-rufous, and underparts are warm buff with narrow black barring on the breast.
funFact: Understory near streams; spectacular fan crest usually hidden—look for cinnamon rump and barred back.
fieldMarks: Warm buff underparts with breast barring
voice: Soft whistles; crest raised when alarmed.

description: A nocturnal bird 38-46 cm (15-18 in) long with grey-brown plumage intricately patterned with black, grey, and cream. It has a large head, long tail, and large yellow eyes. Often seen perched motionless upright on branches or posts, camouflaged as a broken branch.
funFact: Scan fence posts and dead snags at dusk; freezes upright and blends perfectly with bark.
fieldMarks: Large nocturnal bird, cryptic brown pattern
Perches upright like a broken branch

description: A medium-large vulture 53-66 cm (21-26 in) long with a 150-165 cm (59-65 in) wingspan. Plumage is black with a green sheen. The featherless head and neck are pale orange with red or blue areas. It feeds on carrion and locates food by both sight and smell.
funFact: Low-flying vulture of marshes and savanna; pale orange head unlike the King Vulture.
fieldMarks: Pale orange bare head and neck
Flies low over wetlands and savanna

description: A small sparrow 14.5-16.5 cm (5.7-6.5 in) long with a bright orange bill. It has a black crown with a gray central stripe, a wide white supercilium on a black face, olive-green upperparts, and a white throat with a broad black band across the upper breast. Found from Mexico to Peru.
funFact: Forest floor and understory; orange bill and black breast band are unmistakable.
fieldMarks: Black crown with gray central stripe

description: A small flycatcher measuring 12 cm (4.7 in) with an olive-brown head and upper back, blackish wings and tail, and a tawny breast and sides. The face and eye ring are grey, the throat is whitish, and the namesake sulphur-yellow rump patch extends from the lower back to the belly. Found in humid lowland forests from Mexico to Ecuador.
funFact: Fans tail to flash yellow rump near forest streams; often in mixed-species understory flocks.

description: A small seed finch measuring 11.5 cm (4.5 in). The male is almost entirely black with a small white wing patch, while the female is a rich brown all over. It has a notably large bill with a straight culmen, distinguishing it from similar species. Found in shrubby and grassy areas from southern Mexico through Central America to Colombia and Ecuador.
funFact: Grassy edges and scrub; massive bill and white wing patch on black male are distinctive.
fieldMarks: Male all black with small white wing patch

description: A medium-sized tinamou 27-29 cm (11-11.5 in) long with brown upperparts heavily barred blackish on back, rump, and wings. The lowerparts are pale brown with a cinnamon breast, greyer belly, and whitish undertail with dark barring. The head is brown with a prominent buff supercilium. Found from Mexico to Costa Rica in moist lowland and gallery forests.
funFact: Gallery and moist forest; barred upperparts and steam-engine song separate from Little Tinamou.
fieldMarks: Medium brown tinamou with barred back

description: A large turkey-like bird 76-91.5 cm (30-36 in) long, mostly dusky olive brown with a faint greenish or purplish iridescence. The belly and crissum are chestnut, breast and belly feathers have white edges, and the throat has a small red wattle. Found from Mexico through Central America to Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Venezuela.
funFact: Listen for the steam-whistle dawn song in tall forest; often perches high in canopy.
fieldMarks: Dusky olive brown with chestnut belly

description: A colorful trogon 25-29 cm (9.8-11.4 in) long. The male has a bright metallic green crown, nape, back, and rump with a black face and throat, a white band separating the green breast from the bright red belly. The female is olive brown where the male is green with a duller red belly. Found from Mexico through Central America to northern South America.
funFact: Midstory sit-and-wait hunter; white collar between green breast and red belly on male.
fieldMarks: Black face and throat, white collar
Female olive-brown where male is green

description: A secretive forest raptor 31-39 cm (12-15 in) long with dark slate-gray upperparts, white underparts finely barred in black, and a tail tipped with white and marked by narrow white bars. Found from Mexico through Central America to South America, it hunts from concealed perches in mature tropical and secondary forest.
funFact: Secretive forest raptor; fine barring on white underparts unlike Collared Forest-Falcon.
fieldMarks: Tail tipped white with narrow bars

description: A medium-sized oriole about 21.5 cm (8.5 in) long with bold black-and-yellow plumage. Adult males have black wings, tail, shoulders, throat, and face contrasting with a bright yellow back and underparts; females are similar but slightly greener. Common in open pine-oak woodlands and dry scrub throughout Belize and Central America, usually below 900 m elevation.
funFact: Pine-oak woodlands in the north; yellow back contrasts with black wings and tail.
fieldMarks: Black wings, tail, throat, and face

description: A small toucan 29-37 cm (11-15 in) long with mostly green plumage, a white throat, and a distinctive bill with a black mandible and yellow maxilla marked with black and white. Found in humid montane and secondary forest from southern Mexico through Belize and Central America, from near sea level to about 3,000 m elevation.
funFact: Montane and secondary forest; much smaller than Keel-billed Toucan with colorful small bill.
fieldMarks: Bill black below, yellow above with black marks

description: A fairly large foliage-gleaner 18-20 cm (7-8 in) long with dark brown upperparts, a deep buff throat, and dusky-scaled buff breast and belly. Found in lowland rainforest and secondary forest from southern Mexico through western Belize, foraging acrobatically in dense undergrowth while gleaning insects from epiphytes and clumps of dead leaves.
funFact: Pulls prey from dead leaf clumps in undergrowth; often with mixed-species flocks.

description: A small flycatcher 11.5-14 cm (4.5-5.5 in) long with a sepia-brown crown, olive-green upperparts, grayish-olive throat, and yellowish belly. Found in lowland evergreen and secondary forest from southern Mexico through Belize and Central America, usually foraging within about 8 m of the ground in the forest understory to mid-level.
funFact: Understory to mid-level forager; sepia cap and yellow belly distinguish from elaenias.

description: A small forest raptor 29-35 cm (11-14 in) long with a dark gray head, white throat with a central dark stripe, gray upperparts, and rufous breast and barred belly. Found in the interior of mature tropical forest from southern Mexico through Belize and Central America, hunting from a perch and diving for insects and small vertebrates.
funFact: Follows monkey troops in canopy; small size and rufous breast unlike Hook-billed Kite.
fieldMarks: Gray head, white throat with dark stripe

description: A very large cracid 78-100 cm (31-39 in) long and up to 4.8 kg (10.6 lb). Males are black with a curly crest, white belly, and yellow bill knob; females occur in barred, rufous, or black morphs. Found in Neotropical rainforests from eastern Mexico through Belize and Central America, foraging mainly on the ground for fallen fruit and arthropods.
funFact: Walks on forest floor for fallen fruit; male black with yellow knob is unmistakable if seen.
fieldMarks: Very large black male with curly crest
Female barred, rufous, or black morph

description: A small ground-dwelling quail about 25 cm (10 in) long and 300 g, with an orange crest, dark brown upperparts flecked with black and rufous, and olive-brown underparts boldly spotted with white. Found in mountain highlands from 1,000 m to the timberline in dense understory from southern Mexico through Belize and Central America.
funFact: Montane understory coveys; usually escapes on foot—listen for whistled duets at dawn.
fieldMarks: Dark brown upperparts flecked black and rufous

description: A distinctive long-legged duck with a bright pink bill and coral-red legs. Adults are chestnut below with a black belly patch, gray face, and bold white wing stripe visible in flight. Often perches in trees and feeds in shallow wetlands and rice fields.
funFact: Flocks give whistling calls from treetops at dawn and dusk; often seen walking on muddy banks rather than swimming.
fieldMarks: Bright pink bill and coral-red legs
Bold white wing stripe in flight
voice: Clear whistling calls, often from treetops.

description: A large, heavy-bodied duck with a distinctive red facial caruncle on males. Wild-type birds are mostly black with white wing patches; domestic forms vary widely. Perches readily in trees near water.
funFact: Often seen along rivers and village ponds; males show prominent red warty facial skin.
fieldMarks: Dark plumage with white wing patches (wild type)
voice: Low hisses and puffing displays; usually quiet.

description: A small dabbling duck. Breeding males have a white crescent in front of the eye and a speckled blue forewing. Females are mottled brown with a dark eyeline. Common on shallow ponds and flooded fields in winter.
funFact: Look for the white crescent on males and powder-blue shoulder patch in flight.
fieldMarks: Male: white crescent in front of eye
voice: Peeping calls from flocks on wetlands.

description: A small dabbling duck. Males are rich cinnamon-red with a red eye and dark back; females are mottled brown resembling other teal. Found on shallow freshwater marshes and ponds.
funFact: Male cinnamon body and red eye are distinctive among Belize ducks when present.
fieldMarks: Male: rich cinnamon-red body, red eye
Female: warm brown mottling, dark eyeline
Small dabbling duck on shallow wetlands
voice: Soft quacking; less vocal than larger ducks.

description: A distinctive dabbling duck with an oversized spoon-shaped bill. Breeding males have green head, white breast, and chestnut flanks; females are mottled brown with a large bill. Filters food from shallow water by bill sweeping.
funFact: The enormous bill is the best field mark at any distance.
fieldMarks: Male: green head, white breast, chestnut sides
voice: Soft quacking; often silent on wintering grounds.

description: A medium-sized dabbling duck. Males have a green eye patch, whitish crown, and pinkish-brown body; females are gray-brown. Often grazes on land and shows a black-bordered white wing patch in flight.
funFact: Note the pale crown on males and white forewing in flight on flooded fields and lagoons.
fieldMarks: Male: green eye patch, whitish crown
White wing patch with black trailing edge
Short bluish bill with black tip

description: An elegant, slender duck with a long pointed tail. Males have chocolate-brown head, white neck stripe, and gray body; females are mottled brown with a pointed tail. Feeds by dabbling in open shallow wetlands.
funFact: Slender shape and long tail distinguish it from other dabbling ducks in flight.
fieldMarks: Male: brown head, white neck stripe, gray body
voice: Soft whistling calls from males; quacking from females.

description: A compact diving duck. Males are blackish with a white ring on the bill and gray sides; the chestnut neck ring is usually hidden. Females are brown with a white eyering and pale cheek patch.
funFact: Look for the white bill ring on males and peaked head shape when diving on lagoons.
fieldMarks: Male: white ring on bill, black back, gray sides

description: A small diving duck. Males have a black head with purple gloss, black breast, and gray back; females are brown with white at the base of the bill. Often forms large rafts on open water in winter.
funFact: Rafts on open lagoons; male head can look black or purplish depending on light.
fieldMarks: Male: black head, gray back, small size for Aythya
Female: brown with white at bill base
voice: Soft grunts during display; mostly silent in Belize.

description: A long-tailed, chicken-like bird of wooded edges and riparian thickets. Mostly olive-brown with a buff belly and bare red throat skin. Noisy groups run along branches and give loud chorus calls at dawn.
funFact: Listen for raucous chachalaca choruses at dawn in river-edge woodland.
voice: Loud rhythmic chachalaca chorus, especially at dawn.

description: A small round quail of open scrub and field edges. Males have a black throat and eyebrow with buff underparts; females are buffier and more mottled. Usually seen in coveys flushing with whirring wings.
funFact: Coveys flush explosively from roadside scrub; male black throat is distinctive.
fieldMarks: Male: black throat and eyebrow, buff underparts
Female: buff mottling, no black throat
voice: Clear whistled bob-WHITE calls from males.

description: The familiar feral pigeon of towns and villages. Variable plumage but often blue-gray with two dark wing bars and iridescent neck. Strong direct flight on broad wings.
funFact: Common around settlements; wild-type birds show pale wing bars and dark wing tips.
fieldMarks: Broad rounded tail with dark band
voice: Low cooing from rooftops and town squares.
